name: Easter
description: Easter traditions, history, and religious significance

questions.q: Easter commemorates which event in Christian belief?

source.notes: Easter Sunday, three days after Good Friday (the crucifixion).

questions.q: On what day of Holy Week is Jesus said to have been crucified?

source.notes: 'Good' from an older meaning of 'holy.' Also called 'Great Friday' in some traditions.

questions.q: Which council formally fixed the date of Easter in 325 AD?

source.notes: First Sunday after the first full moon on or after the vernal equinox.

questions.q: The word 'Easter' is believed to derive from the name of an Anglo-Saxon goddess of what?

source.notes: From 'Eostre' or 'Eostrae.' First recorded by the Venerable Bede.

questions.q: What type of meat is most traditionally associated with Easter meals in the UK/US?

source.notes: Lamb symbolizes Jesus (Lamb of God). Ham became popular in the US due to spring pig slaughter timing.

questions.q: Hot Cross Buns are traditionally eaten on which day?

source.notes: The cross represents Jesus's crucifixion. Sweet, spiced buns with currants or raisins.

questions.q: In the Easter story, who sentenced Jesus to crucifixion?

source.notes: Roman prefect of Judea. Famously 'washed his hands' of the decision.

questions.q: What famous tradition during Easter involves children searching for something hidden in gardens?

source.notes: Likely derives from Germanic pagan traditions of egg hunting for fertility.

questions.q: What kind of eggs did Imperial Russia's Fabergé workshop famously create for Tsars Alexander III and Nicholas II for Easter?

source.notes: Peter Carl Fabergé. 50 imperial eggs created 1885-1917. Each contained a 'surprise' inside.

questions.q: Easter Monday is a public holiday in many Christian countries. Which famous water fight tradition occurs in Poland on this day?

source.notes: 'Wet Monday.' Tradition of splashing water on each other. Also practiced in other Slavic countries.

questions.q: What period of 40 days precedes Easter in Christian tradition?

source.notes: Marks Jesus's 40 days of fasting in the wilderness. Begins on Ash Wednesday.

questions.q: What day immediately precedes Ash Wednesday (and Lent) in many countries?
choices: Shrove Tuesday / Mardi Gras / Fat Tuesday

source.notes: Called 'Pancake Day' in the UK. Mardi Gras ('Fat Tuesday') in French-speaking regions.

questions.q: Which Orthodox Christian country is famous for its 'Pascha' Easter bread and red-dyed eggs?

source.notes: Orthodox Easter follows Julian calendar — often falls a week or more after Western Easter.

questions.q: What is the name of the tradition where Catholics wave palm branches the Sunday before Easter?

source.notes: Commemorates Jesus's triumphant entry into Jerusalem. Palms often folded into crosses.

questions.q: The Last Supper, a key part of the Easter story, is commemorated on what day?

source.notes: 'Maundy' from Latin 'mandatum' (command) — Jesus's new commandment to love one another.

questions.q: Which holiday, often around Easter time, celebrates the Jewish people's liberation from slavery in Egypt?

source.notes: 'Pesach' in Hebrew. Jesus's Last Supper was a Passover seder.

questions.q: What kind of flowers are traditionally associated with Easter, especially in North America?

source.notes: Easter Lily (Lilium longiflorum). White trumpet-shaped. Symbolizes purity and resurrection.

questions.q: In the Easter story, which disciple betrayed Jesus for 30 pieces of silver?

source.notes: Identified Jesus to the authorities with a kiss. Later hanged himself out of guilt.

questions.q: On the first Easter morning, who first discovered Jesus's empty tomb?

source.notes: Gospels of Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John all name Mary Magdalene (sometimes with other women).

questions.q: In the US, approximately how many jelly beans are eaten during the Easter season?

source.notes: 16 billion jelly beans sold for Easter in the US alone. Most popular flavor: cherry.
